workflowProgress.resultPreview: Chroma (the open-source vector store) uses SQLite as its default persistence backend in persistent/single-node mode. When you instantiate a persistent client (e.g. `chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")`), Chroma writes a `chroma.sqlite3` file into the target directory. QUESTION: How does SQLite's write concurrency compare to PostgreSQL under concurrent writers, per documented behavior?
Date context: today is 2026-07-01; treat "recent/current/as of 2026" relative to that date.

Do a quick job: at most 2 WebSearch c…
workflowProgress.resultPreview: SQLite serializes all writes: at most one writer at a time. Per SQLite's official docs, even in WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) mode — its most concurrent mode — "there can only be one writer at a time" because there is only one WAL file. WAL lets readers and the single writer proceed concurrently (readers don't block the writer, the writer doesn't block readers, each reader gets snapshot isolation), bu…

BUCKET: B4-nfr   EXPECTED-SHAPE of a decision-useful answer: mechanism (single-writer lock vs MVCC) +…
workflowProgress.resultPreview:
SQLite serializes ALL writes to a database; PostgreSQL serializes only writes that touch the SAME ROW.

Mechanism — SQLite: a single global write lock per database file. Per the official WAL docs, "since there is only one WAL file, there can only be one writer at a time." Readers and one writer can run concurrently ("readers do not block writers and a writer does not block readers"), but write tra…
